nativeRange.habitat: Juveniles inhabit estuaries, mangrove creeks, tidal rivers, and the lower freshwater reaches of coastal rivers; as they mature, adults migrate to coastal reef flats and open marine waters.

decorPreferences: open water for shoaling and active swimming
robust rock or coral rubble decor (salt-resistant)
minimal or no live plants (incompatible with brackish/marine transition)
brackish-tolerant artificial plants as cover if desired

diet.notes: The Mono accepts a wide variety of foods, including high-quality marine flake and pellets, frozen brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, bloodworms, and chopped seafood. It will also graze on algae and plant matter. Feeding two to three times daily in small amounts is generally recommended; a varied diet supports the best coloration and immune health, particularly during the brackish-to-marine transition.

goodWith: robust brackish-tolerant cichlids (early juvenile phase only)
other euryhaline species sharing the salinity progression
avoidWith: small or delicate freshwater fish (incompatible long-term salinity needs)
purely freshwater species once brackish transition begins
overly aggressive marines when first introduced to full marine

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo gallery for Monodactylus argenteus (species ID 879). Multiple images available; reviewer should select best lateral view of adult specimen showing diamond profile.
Wikimedia Commons category for Monodactylus argenteus; several freely licensed images of wild and aquarium specimens.

summary: The Mono (Monodactylus argenteus) is a diamond-shaped Indo-Pacific fish commonly sold as a juvenile in freshwater sections of aquarium stores, though it naturally progresses through brackish water and into full marine conditions as it matures. Keeping it long-term requires a deliberate salinity transition and a large tank — this is not a species suited to a typical freshwater community aquarium.
careNotes:
The Mono is a euryhaline fish — meaning it can tolerate a wide range of salinities — but this adaptability comes with a long-term husbandry commitment that many buyers do not anticipate when they first encounter juveniles in the freshwater section of a retail store. In nature, Monos begin life in estuarine and mangrove nursery habitats and gradually migrate to coastal reef environments as they mature. Long-term care requires a deliberate salinity progression to match this life history: juveniles are typically started at low brackish conditions (specific gravity 1.003–1.005) and moved incrementally over months to years toward SG 1.010 and above for sub-adults, ultimately reaching full marine conditions (SG 1.020–1.025) for adult fish. Fish kept indefinitely in pure freshwater generally show declining health, elevated disease susceptibility, and shortened lifespan.

A group of at least four is generally advisable; lone or paired specimens tend to become stressed and may hide or display aggression toward tankmates. Adults reaching 25 cm or more in a shoal require a tank of at least 380 liters (approximately 100 US gallons) — and ideally considerably more — with a long horizontal footprint to accommodate their active, open-water swimming style. Filtration must be robust, as Monos are messy feeders.

Most aquatic plants cannot survive the brackish and marine conditions these fish ultimately require, so décor is best planned around salt-tolerant hardscape such as rock or coral rubble. Water temperature should remain stable between 24 and 28°C throughout all life stages. Monos are active midwater swimmers and benefit from open swimming lanes; a tight-fitting lid is advisable, as they can leap when startled.

Copper-based medications are best avoided. While their copper sensitivity has not been definitively confirmed, the standard approach for euryhaline and marine fish is to use alternatives such as hyposalinity or formalin-based treatments.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Monodactylus argenteus has not been reliably documented in aquarium literature. In the wild, spawning is believed to occur in coastal marine or reef-associated waters. Eggs and larvae are planktonic. Sexing is not possible by external examination. No practical captive breeding protocol exists; specimens available in the trade are wild-caught or farm-raised juveniles harvested from estuarine nursery areas.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Monodactylus argenteus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed adult size (to 27 cm), temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.0–8.3, native range (Indo-Pacific, East Africa to Australia), estuarine-to-marine life history, shoaling requirement (groups of 4+), semi-aggressive temperament, and the critical salinity progression detail (juvenile freshwater/brackish → adult marine). Diet confirmed as omnivore accepting flake, frozen, and live foods.

additional.name: FishBase — Monodactylus argenteus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (reported to 27 cm TL), distribution map confirming Indo-Pacific and East African coastal range, taxonomy (Monodactylidae, Perciformes), depth range (0–20 m), habitat notes (estuaries, coastal reefs), and euryhaline classification. Temperature range 24–28°C confirmed.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mono Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care overview. Confirmed brackish-to-marine transition requirement, minimum group size of 4+, large tank requirement (100+ gal), omnivore diet, compatibility notes with scats and archerfish. Highlighted the retail freshwater labeling trap and long-term salinity progression commitment.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Monodactylus argenteus species profile
additional.notes: UK trade source. Corroborated the estuarine juvenile / reef adult life history, salinity requirement progression, large adult size (to 25 cm in captivity), and the recommendation to keep with other euryhaline species (scats, archerfish). Noted fin-nipping tendency toward slow or long-finned tankmates.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Brackish Fishes: The Mono
additional.notes: In-depth hobbyist article detailing the salinity progression husbandry protocol, water chemistry across life stages, compatibility with scats and other estuarine species, and notes on captive longevity (7–10 years with proper marine transition). Confirmed no reliable captive breeding records.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding protocol has been documented. In the wild, spawning is believed to occur in coastal marine waters, with eggs and larvae that drift freely in the water column. The sexes are indistinguishable by external examination. Captive specimens are wild-caught or estuarine farm-raised juveniles.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Sold as freshwater fish but is BRACKISH→MARINE at adult (similar to Colombian Shark Catfish fw-fish-304 trap). Long-term keeping requires transition to brackish (SG 1.010+) then marine. Specialist euryhaline husbandry commitment.
